publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 166, folios 74-75. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: I was so amazed when I read the beginning of your letter this morning, at the absolute identity of the principle expressed in it, to that which I had expressed (tho' in a different form) to you in my last, that I could not help exclaiming to Babbage "this is most extraordinary"! & relating to him the circumstance.
There are however so many very remarkable circumstances that have occurred during the

latter weeks & months, & which I cannot pretend to unravel at present, but which something tells me I shall find the real clue to all in good time, that this merely adds one to their number. I am very much afraid as yet of exerting the powers I know I have over others, & the evidence of which [three words crossed out] [two words crossed out] I have certainly been most unwilling to admit, & in fact for a long time considered to be quite fanciful & absurd. I am an utter novice at present, on the threshold of a new world; & I feel my best plan is to wait modestly & humbly for God to teach me, & not to anticipate that teaching, but rather to keep behind even. I therefore carefully refrain from

all attempts intentionally to exercise unusual powers. Those powers are not ripe, & my habits & principles are still far too young, raw, & green, for me to be wise [three words crossed out] in venturing beyond a certain pale as yet.

I had better continue to be simply the High-Priestess of Babbage's Engine, & serve my apprenticeship faithfully therein, before I fancy myself worthy to approach a step higher towards being the High-Priestess of God Almighty Himself. And one has a fearful universe of habits to acquire. Many get habits first, & great principles afterwards. But here am I (who always did everything topsy- -turvy, & certainly ought to have come into the world feet downwards),
with principles highly & largely developped, but habits alas that are either non-existent or positively bad, (& hardly a single good one).
So one must drag one's habits (however unwillingly), after & up to one's principles, before one thinks oneself worth anything whatever.--

By the way, I am particularly ill at present. In fact I have been getting ill now for 3 or 4 weeks, & I suppose I shall continue queer for 3 or 4 more; & then I shall be very well again for a time no doubt. And so on, for perhaps a year or two more. But the time will come, when all this will be different.
I suffer a great deal I believe, but I am well satisfied with things & their aspect, except just at
[text written sideways on page 1 to the left of the heading] moments, when some actual sensation seems beyong all toleration. But then it is sure to go off.
[text written below heading on page 1 and circled by a curved line] P.S. I do much suspect V. Beaurepaire is insane. Did W-- mention to you the prostitution letter?
